which itself is a multidisciplinary panel
of interest in professionals and service providers for a CC.
Now, I was watching some of the earlier submissions,
and I noted that the question of
where exactly is this inequity kept popping up.
So I just wanted to provide an example, a practical example
of what exactly that might be.
So let's use the example of persons X and Y.
Now, both of these people were injured at the same time,
and a CC coverable circumstances, person
X receives their a CC cover and Person Y does not.
And this state of affair continues for five years
and will identify those five years as starting at point A,
being the date of injury
and ending at point B,
being the date when person wise cover is recognized.
And at that stage, at point B, both
of them are on equal footing.
Now, person X who does have a CC cover receives
as their main benefit.
So we'll usually terms of the bill here for clarity, uh, low
or loss of potential earnings set at 80% of minimum wage.
And they receive that for the full five years.
Now, as their supplementary entitlements, they receive,
for example, rehabilitation in the form of, uh, therapy
or of physiotherapy or other clinical interventions.
They receive vocational rehab so that they can either return
to their pre-injury role
or adapt to their new normal and find a new role.
And they can receive adjunct
or supplementary entitlements such as, uh, transportation,
home health, even educational support.
And that's because they were appropriately assessed
and were triaged into the correct, uh, ministerial role.
Uh, plus, and y on the other hand, was subject
to an era of some kind.
They did not receive a CC support when they should have,
and it took those five years between point A
and point B for that to be established.
Now, for those five years,
that person received the main benefit or,
or living systems under MSD to the tune of 52%
of the minimum wage.
And their supplementary benefits were, say, accommodation,
which you only get if you need it.
Winter energy support,
which I understand isn't means tested,
but amounts only to about $500 a year.
And due to the type of injury they suffered, uh, access
to disability support, which with which they might pay
for their own medication, transport therapy, et cetera.
And as I say, this continues for five years.
And the resolution here is what we're really concerned
with is at point B when both persons are now viewed
as equal under the law.
Person X who received their support the whole time
as they were due, has been rehabilitated.
They've been receiving this higher amount the whole time.
They have likely returned to work or
otherwise been sufficiently rehabilitated
to return to an alternative role.
But person y who has been the unfortunate victim of an error
and has been receiving a lower quantum for those four,
five years, is essentially starting
to gain from the beginning.
They are now receiving their rehabilitation support.
They're now receiving their vocational entitlements.
And this bill seeks to treat those people retrospectively,
as always having been on the same footing.
And that, I think is the inequity that is being highlighted
by the submitters, is that regardless of whether
or not the bill itself applies retrospectively,
the conclusion to the bill will always
inherently be retrospective.
It is deeming a state of affairs that did not, in fact occur
to have occurred over a period of time
and punishing the persons, um, not
entitled at first instance, to a CC support
for their inability to either tell the future
or to convince a ministry without years of
often quite difficult litigation
that they were in fact entitled to it in the first place.
Now, that actually brings me to my second point, which is
that this highlights an inconsistency
between the way the proposed bill administers, um, with,
with regard to errors
and the way the current legislation does.
So let's view the reverse situation where somebody
who should not have received a CC cover does in fact get it.
If they had a congenital mental injury, for example,
that was misdiagnosed as a consequence of trauma
and cover was awarded,
they could access this vastly more expensive rehabilitation.
They could access all
of these supplementary assistances such as, uh, education

to keep it specifically
because there was no element of wrongdoing on their part.

no fault of the recipient, they do
nonetheless receive save in a surgical entitlement

to the tune of several hundreds of thousands of dollars, uh,
unless there was an element of wrongdoing,
unless there was fraud or misleading, they get to keep that.
And there is no rational reason why a more injured party

resolution of substantially similar proceedings,
be disadvantaged by this bill, particularly when
that comes inherently retrospective effect,
not only built into the bill,
but into the actual structure

Um, I just wanna play like flip this around a bit
and sort of play semi devil's advocate.
Do you think then, if this was to go ahead,
that a CC should be paying or covering some of those costs?
'cause some of the other submitters said, you know,
a CC covers, you know, rehabilitation
or, um, you know, mental health, all those kind of things.
Um, and that is the sort of stuff that people would've used,
maybe their disability allowance or,
or some of their extra supplementary, um, benefits
that they now need to pay back.
Um, do you think then that when a CC is giving this kind
of compensation that then maybe there should be an element
of that if it is that MSD needs to recoup it?
You mean that, uh, is your question Laura,
that a CC should be the agency that reimburses MD
Correct. Yeah.
Not the individual. I'm
Just, yeah, I think Taking it as a way Yeah,
Yeah. Yeah. I, i look,
I think there is some merit in that,
although it, because why, why I kind of prefaced
how a CC goes about sorting, you know, deciding whether
or not a person needs social
rehabilitation or even treatment.
Mm. There's quite a process there with a, a fair bit
of exercise of discretion so that they say,
does the person need it?
Right, okay. Uh, does, is it needed for that covered injury?
Uh, have they been assessed appropriately?
Um, have I, can I give it prior approval?
In other words, not retrospective.
So if I'm a person who was so desperate, which is one
of my clients currently so desperate to keep his job,
that he paid for the surgery, he got no prior approval,
that I will then have a bit of a problem
of getting him reimbursed.
Uh, yeah. For that it's
because there's certain rules, uh, I think it would,
a CC would have to change its processes.
Mm-hmm. And God talk about bureaucracy. Yeah. Yeah.
Don't wanna open another can of worms, but Yeah.
Yeah. What's your ministry
of regulations supposed to be doing?
Yeah, totally. Well, I think it would be a headache.
Absolutely. Cool. Thank you. Thank
You. Well, thank, thank you very much.
It's, um, been, been, um,
been helpful I think for the committee
to hear from this afternoon. So I appreciate that.
Yeah. I really appreciate the opportunity.
Thank you very much. Thank
You.
Alright, that concludes, um, public submission.
So members have just can, um, go
and click on the private link and we'll see you back.
Um, for consideration, I.
